   103	impl LogicalFileView {
   104	    /// Creates a new view into the specified file entry.
   105	    pub(crate) fn new(files: RecordBatch, index: usize) -> Self {
   106	        Self { files, index }
   107	    }
   108	
   109	    /// Returns the file path with URL decoding applied.
   110	    pub fn path(&self) -> Cow<'_, str> {
   111	        let raw = get_string_value(
   112	            self.files
   113	                .column(*FIELD_INDICES.get(FIELD_NAME_PATH).unwrap()),
   114	            self.index,
   115	        )
   116	        .unwrap();
   117	        percent_decode_str(raw).decode_utf8_lossy()
   118	    }
   119	
   120	    /// Returns the raw file path as stored in the log, without URL decoding.
   121	    pub(crate) fn path_raw(&self) -> &str {
   122	        get_string_value(
   123	            self.files
   124	                .column(*FIELD_INDICES.get(FIELD_NAME_PATH).unwrap()),
   125	            self.index,
   126	        )
   127	        .unwrap()
   128	    }
   129	
   130	    /// An object store [`Path`] to the file.
   131	    ///
   132	    /// this tries to parse the file string and if that fails, it will return the string as is.
   133	    // TODO assert consistent handling of the paths encoding when reading log data so this logic can be removed.
   134	    pub(crate) fn object_store_path(&self) -> Path {
   135	        let path = self.path();
   136	        // Try to preserve percent encoding if possible
   137	        match Path::parse(path.as_ref()) {
   138	            Ok(path) => path,
   139	            Err(_) => Path::from(path.as_ref()),
   140	        }

    91	/// Provides semantic, typed access to file metadata from Delta log replay.
    92	///
    93	/// This struct wraps a RecordBatch containing file data and provides zero-copy
    94	/// access to individual file entries through an index. It serves as a view into
    95	/// the kernel's log replay results, offering convenient methods to extract
    96	/// file properties without unnecessary data copies.
    97	#[derive(Clone)]
    98	pub struct LogicalFileView {
    99	    files: RecordBatch,
   100	    index: usize,
   101	}

   107	    }

     1	use std::sync::Arc;

     3	use arrow_array::RecordBatch;
     4	use delta_kernel::actions::{Metadata, Protocol};
     5	use delta_kernel::expressions::{Scalar, StructData};

    10	use super::super::scalars::ScalarExt;

    12	
    13	pub(crate) trait PartitionsExt {
    14	    fn hive_partition_path(&self) -> String;
    15	}
    16	
    17	impl PartitionsExt for IndexMap<String, Scalar> {
    18	    fn hive_partition_path(&self) -> String {
    19	        let sep = String::from('/');
    20	        itertools::Itertools::intersperse(
    21	            self.iter().map(|(k, v)| {
    22	                let encoded = v.serialize_encoded();
    23	                format!("{k}={encoded}")
    24	            }),
    25	            sep,
    26	        )
    27	        .collect()
    28	    }
    29	}
    30	
    31	impl PartitionsExt for StructData {
    32	    fn hive_partition_path(&self) -> String {
    33	        let sep = String::from('/');
    34	        itertools::Itertools::intersperse(
    35	            self.fields()
    36	                .iter()
    37	                .zip(self.values().iter())
    38	                .map(|(k, v)| {
    39	                    let encoded = v.serialize_encoded();
    40	                    format!("{}={encoded}", k.name())
    41	                }),
    42	            sep,
    43	        )
    44	        .collect()
    45	    }
    46	}
    47	
    48	impl<T: PartitionsExt> PartitionsExt for Arc<T> {
    49	    fn hive_partition_path(&self) -> String {
    50	        self.as_ref().hive_partition_path()
    51	    }
    52	}
    53	
    54	/// Provides semanitc access to the log data.
    55	///
    56	/// This is a helper struct that provides access to the log data in a more semantic way
    57	/// to avid the necessiity of knowing the exact layout of the underlying log data.
    58	#[derive(Clone)]
    59	pub struct LogDataHandler<'a> {
    60	    data: &'a [RecordBatch],
    61	    config: &'a TableConfiguration,
    62	}
    63	
    64	impl<'a> LogDataHandler<'a> {
    65	    pub(crate) fn new(data: &'a [RecordBatch], config: &'a TableConfiguration) -> Self {
    66	        Self { data, config }
    67	    }
    68	
    69	    pub(crate) fn table_configuration(&self) -> &TableConfiguration {
    70	        self.config
    71	    }
    72	
    73	    pub(crate) fn table_properties(&self) -> &TableProperties {
    74	        self.config.table_properties()
    75	    }
    76	
    77	    pub(crate) fn protocol(&self) -> &Protocol {
    78	        self.config.protocol()
    79	    }
    80	
    81	    pub(crate) fn metadata(&self) -> &Metadata {
    82	        self.config.metadata()
    83	    }
    84	
    85	    /// The number of files in the log data.
    86	    pub fn num_files(&self) -> usize {
    87	        self.data.iter().map(|batch| batch.num_rows()).sum()
    88	    }
    89	
    90	    pub fn iter(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = LogicalFileView> + '_ {
    91	        self.data.iter().flat_map(|batch| {
    92	            (0..batch.num_rows()).map(move |idx| LogicalFileView::new(batch.clone(), idx))
    93	        })
    94	    }
    95	}
    96	
    97	impl IntoIterator for LogDataHandler<'_> {
    98	    type Item = LogicalFileView;
    99	    type IntoIter = Box<dyn Iterator<Item = Self::Item>>;
   100	
   101	    fn into_iter(self) -> Self::IntoIter {
   102	        let owned = self.data.to_vec();
   103	        Box::new(owned.into_iter().flat_map(|batch| {
   104	            (0..batch.num_rows()).map(move |idx| LogicalFileView::new(batch.clone(), idx))
   105	        }))
   106	    }
   107	}
   108	
   109	#[cfg(feature = "datafusion")]
   110	mod datafusion {
   111	    use std::collections::HashSet;
   112	    use std::sync::{Arc, LazyLock};
   113	
   114	    use ::datafusion::common::Column;
   115	    use ::datafusion::common::scalar::ScalarValue;
   116	    use ::datafusion::physical_optimizer::pruning::PruningStatistics;
   117	    use arrow::compute::concat;
   118	    use arrow_array::{Array, StringArray};
   119	    use arrow_array::{ArrayRef, BooleanArray, UInt64Array};
   120	    use arrow_schema::DataType as ArrowDataType;
   121	    use delta_kernel::expressions::Expression;
   122	    use delta_kernel::schema::{DataType, PrimitiveType};
   123	    use delta_kernel::{EvaluationHandler, ExpressionEvaluator};
   124	    use itertools::Itertools;
   125	
   126	    use super::*;
   127	    use crate::kernel::arrow::engine_ext::ExpressionEvaluatorExt as _;
   128	    use crate::{DeltaResult, DeltaTableError};
   129	
   130	    use crate::kernel::ARROW_HANDLER;
   131	
   132	    impl LogDataHandler<'_> {
   133	        fn pick_stats(&self, column: &Column, stats_field: &'static str) -> Option<ArrayRef> {
   134	            let schema = self.config.logical_schema();
   135	            let field = schema.field(&column.name)?;
   136	            // See issue #1214. Binary type does not support natural order which is required for Datafusion to prune
   137	            if field.data_type() == &DataType::Primitive(PrimitiveType::Binary) {
   138	                return None;
   139	            }
   140	            let expression = if self
   141	                .config
   142	                .metadata()
   143	                .partition_columns()
   144	                .contains(&column.name)
   145	            {
   146	                Expression::column(["partitionValues_parsed", &column.name])
   147	            } else {
   148	                Expression::column(["stats_parsed", stats_field, &column.name])
   149	            };
   150	            let evaluator = ARROW_HANDLER
   151	                .new_expression_evaluator(
   152	                    crate::kernel::models::fields::log_schema_ref().clone(),
   153	                    expression.into(),
   154	                    field.data_type().clone(),
   155	                )
   156	                .ok()?;
   157	
   158	            let results: Vec<_> = self
   159	                .data
   160	                .iter()
   161	                .map(|data| -> DeltaResult<_> {
   162	                    Ok(evaluator.evaluate_arrow(data.clone())?.column(0).clone())
   163	                })
   164	                .try_collect()
   165	                .ok()?;
   166	            concat(
   167	                &results
   168	                    .iter()
   169	                    .map(|result| result.as_ref())
   170	                    .collect::<Vec<_>>(),
   171	            )
   172	            .ok()
   173	        }
   174	    }
   175	
   176	    impl PruningStatistics for LogDataHandler<'_> {
   177	        /// return the minimum values for the named column, if known.
   178	        /// Note: the returned array must contain `num_containers()` rows
   179	        fn min_values(&self, column: &Column) -> Option<ArrayRef> {
   180	            self.pick_stats(column, "minValues")
   181	        }
   182	
   183	        /// return the maximum values for the named column, if known.
   184	        /// Note: the returned array must contain `num_containers()` rows.
   185	        fn max_values(&self, column: &Column) -> Option<ArrayRef> {
   186	            self.pick_stats(column, "maxValues")
   187	        }
   188	
   189	        /// return the number of containers (e.g. row groups) being
   190	        /// pruned with these statistics
   191	        fn num_containers(&self) -> usize {
   192	            self.data.iter().map(|b| b.num_rows()).sum()
   193	        }
   194	
   195	        /// return the number of null values for the named column as an
   196	        /// `Option<UInt64Array>`.
   197	        ///
   198	        /// Note: the returned array must contain `num_containers()` rows.
   199	        fn null_counts(&self, column: &Column) -> Option<ArrayRef> {
   200	            if !self
   201	                .config
   202	                .metadata()
   203	                .partition_columns()
   204	                .contains(&column.name)
   205	            {
   206	                let counts = self.pick_stats(column, "nullCount")?;
   207	                return arrow_cast::cast(counts.as_ref(), &ArrowDataType::UInt64).ok();
   208	            }
   209	            let partition_values = self.pick_stats(column, "__dummy__")?;
   210	            let row_counts = self.row_counts(column)?;
   211	            let row_counts = row_counts.as_any().downcast_ref::<UInt64Array>()?;
   212	            let mut null_counts = Vec::with_capacity(partition_values.len());
   213	            for i in 0..partition_values.len() {
   214	                let null_count = if partition_values.is_null(i) {
   215	                    row_counts.value(i)
   216	                } else {
   217	                    0
   218	                };
   219	                null_counts.push(null_count);
   220	            }
   221	            Some(Arc::new(UInt64Array::from(null_counts)))
   222	        }
   223	
   224	        /// return the number of rows for the named column in each container
   225	        /// as an `Option<UInt64Array>`.
   226	        ///
   227	        /// Note: the returned array must contain `num_containers()` rows
   228	        fn row_counts(&self, _column: &Column) -> Option<ArrayRef> {
   229	            static ROW_COUNTS_EVAL: LazyLock<Arc<dyn ExpressionEvaluator>> = LazyLock::new(|| {
   230	                ARROW_HANDLER
   231	                    .new_expression_evaluator(
   232	                        crate::kernel::models::fields::log_schema_ref().clone(),
   233	                        Expression::column(["add", "stats_parsed", "numRecords"]).into(),
   234	                        DataType::Primitive(PrimitiveType::Long),
   235	                    )
   236	                    .expect("Failed to create row counts evaluator")
   237	            });
   238	
   239	            let results: Vec<_> = self
   240	                .data
   241	                .iter()
   242	                .map(|data| -> DeltaResult<_> {
   243	                    let batch = ROW_COUNTS_EVAL.evaluate_arrow(data.clone())?;
   244	                    Ok(arrow_cast::cast(batch.column(0), &ArrowDataType::UInt64)?)
   245	                })
   246	                .try_collect()
   247	                .ok()?;
   248	
   249	            concat(
   250	                &results
   251	                    .iter()
   252	                    .map(|result| result.as_ref())
   253	                    .collect::<Vec<_>>(),
   254	            )
   255	            .ok()
   256	        }
   257	
   258	        // This function is optional but will optimize partition column pruning
   259	        fn contained(&self, column: &Column, value: &HashSet<ScalarValue>) -> Option<BooleanArray> {
   260	            if value.is_empty()
   261	                || !self
   262	                    .config
   263	                    .metadata()
   264	                    .partition_columns()
   265	                    .contains(&column.name)
   266	            {
   267	                return None;
   268	            }
   269	
   270	            // Retrieve the partition values for the column
   271	            let partition_values = self.pick_stats(column, "__dummy__")?;
   272	
   273	            let partition_values = partition_values
   274	                .as_any()
   275	                .downcast_ref::<StringArray>()
   276	                .ok_or(DeltaTableError::generic(
   277	                    "failed to downcast string result to StringArray.",
   278	                ))
   279	                .ok()?;
   280	
   281	            let mut contains = Vec::with_capacity(partition_values.len());
   282	
   283	            // TODO: this was inspired by parquet's BloomFilter pruning, decide if we should
   284	            //  just convert to Vec<String> for a subset of column types and use .contains
   285	            fn check_scalar(pv: &str, value: &ScalarValue) -> bool {
   286	                match value {
   287	                    ScalarValue::Utf8(Some(v))
   288	                    | ScalarValue::Utf8View(Some(v))
   289	                    | ScalarValue::LargeUtf8(Some(v)) => pv == v,
   290	
   291	                    ScalarValue::Dictionary(_, inner) => check_scalar(pv, inner),
   292	                    // FIXME: is this a good enough default or should we sync this with
   293	                    //  expr_applicable_for_cols and bail out with None
   294	                    _ => value.to_string() == pv,
   295	                }
   296	            }
   297	
   298	            for i in 0..partition_values.len() {
   299	                if partition_values.is_null(i) {
   300	                    contains.push(false);
   301	                } else {
   302	                    contains.push(
   303	                        value
   304	                            .iter()
   305	                            .any(|scalar| check_scalar(partition_values.value(i), scalar)),
   306	                    );
   307	                }
   308	            }
   309	
   310	            Some(BooleanArray::from(contains))
   311	        }
   312	    }
   313	}
   314	
   315	#[cfg(test)]
   316	mod tests {
   317	    use super::*;
   318	    use delta_kernel::schema::DataType;
   319	    use delta_kernel::schema::PrimitiveType;
   320	    use delta_kernel::schema::StructField;
   321	    use pretty_assertions::assert_eq;
   322	
   323	    #[test]
   324	    fn test_partitionsext_structdata() {
   325	        let partitions = StructData::try_new(
   326	            vec![
   327	                StructField::new("year", DataType::LONG, false),
   328	                StructField::new("month", DataType::LONG, false),
   329	                StructField::new("day", DataType::LONG, false),
   330	            ],
   331	            vec![Scalar::Long(2025), Scalar::Long(1), Scalar::Long(1)],
   332	        )
   333	        .expect("Failed to make StructData");
   334	        assert_eq!("year=2025/month=1/day=1", partitions.hive_partition_path());
   335	
   336	        let partitions = StructData::try_new(
   337	            vec![StructField::new("year", DataType::LONG, true)],
   338	            vec![Scalar::Null(DataType::Primitive(PrimitiveType::Long))],
   339	        )
   340	        .expect("Failed to make StructData");
   341	        assert_eq!(
   342	            "year=__HIVE_DEFAULT_PARTITION__",
   343	            partitions.hive_partition_path()
   344	        );
   345	    }
   346	
   347	    #[test]
   348	    fn test_partitionsext_indexmap() {
   349	        let partitions: IndexMap<String, Scalar> = IndexMap::from([
   350	            ("year".to_string(), Scalar::Long(2025)),
   351	            ("month".to_string(), Scalar::Long(1)),
   352	            ("day".to_string(), Scalar::Long(1)),
   353	        ]);

Now I see that `LogDataHandler` iterates over `LogicalFileView` items. And `LogicalFileView::path()` (line 110-118) does `percent_decode_str(raw).decode_utf8_lossy()`.

So when the merge code does:
```rust
for action in snapshot.log_data() {
    let rel_path = action.path();
    let rel_path_str = rel_path.as_ref();
    if should_remove_rewritten_file(&survivors, rel_path_str, &table_root)? {
```

`rel_path_str` is the percent-decoded path. But the survivors set contains full URLs from `file_url.as_str()` which were constructed using `path_raw()` (NOT percent-decoded) joined with the table root.

Let me verify this. Let me look at `parse_path` and how `path_raw()` is used:

In `extract_file_statistics` (replay.rs line 370):
```rust
Some((
    parse_path(scan.snapshot().table_root(), view.path_raw()).ok()?,
    ...
))
```

`path_raw()` returns the raw path (NOT percent-decoded). Then `parse_path` joins it with the table root URL. Since the path is not percent-decoded and may contain spaces, `Url::parse` will fail, and `url.join(path)` will percent-encode the space.

But then in `should_remove_rewritten_file`:
```rust
fn should_remove_rewritten_file(
    survivors: &barrier::BarrierSurvivorSet,
    rel_path: &str,
    table_root: &url::Url,
) -> DeltaResult<bool> {
    if survivors.contains(rel_path) {
        return Ok(true);
    }
    // Compare against normalized file IDs.
    let full_id = normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, "merge remove")?;
    Ok(survivors.contains(full_id.as_str()))
}
```

Where `rel_path` is from `action.path()` which is percent-decoded. So:
1. `survivors.contains(rel_path)` - This checks if the percent-decoded relative path is in survivors. But survivors contain full URLs, so this fails.
2. `normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, ...)` - This joins the percent-decoded path with the table root. If the path contains spaces, the URL join will encode them. But wait, `rel_path` is already percent-decoded! So it has actual spaces. `url.join(path)` will percent-encode them to `%20`. 

But survivors were built using `path_raw()` which is NOT percent-decoded. So if the original path in the Add action was something like `group=foo%20bar/part-0000.parquet` (already encoded), then `path_raw()` returns `group=foo%20bar/part-0000.parquet` and the full URL becomes `.../group=foo%20bar/part-0000.parquet`.

But `action.path()` percent-decodes it, giving `group=foo bar/part-0000.parquet`. Then `normalize_path_as_file_id` joins this with the table root, producing `.../group=foo%20bar/part-0000.parquet` (the same). So they should match!

Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the issue is that paths in the Add actions are stored WITHOUT percent encoding? Let me check how data is written. Let me look at how file paths are constructed when writing data:

   315	    pub fn try_new(
   316	        file_schema: ArrowSchemaRef,

   319	        target_file_size: Option<NonZeroU64>,

   321	        max_concurrency_tasks: Option<usize>,

     1	use std::cmp::min;
     2	use std::ops::Not;
     3	use std::sync::Arc;
     4	use std::time::{SystemTime, UNIX_EPOCH};
     5	use std::{collections::HashMap, ops::AddAssign};
     6	
     7	use delta_kernel::expressions::Scalar;
     8	use delta_kernel::table_properties::DataSkippingNumIndexedCols;
     9	use indexmap::IndexMap;
    10	use itertools::Itertools;
    11	use parquet::basic::LogicalType;
    12	use parquet::basic::Type;
    13	use parquet::file::metadata::ParquetMetaData;
    14	use parquet::schema::types::{ColumnDescriptor, SchemaDescriptor};
    15	use parquet::{
    16	    basic::TimeUnit,
    17	    file::{metadata::RowGroupMetaData, statistics::Statistics},
    18	};

    24	
    25	/// Creates an [`Add`] log action struct.
    26	pub(crate) fn create_add(
    27	    partition_values: &IndexMap<String, Scalar>,
    28	    path: String,
    29	    size: i64,
    30	    file_metadata: &ParquetMetaData,
    31	    num_indexed_cols: DataSkippingNumIndexedCols,
    32	    stats_columns: &Option<Vec<impl AsRef<str>>>,
    33	) -> Result<Add, DeltaTableError> {
    34	    let stats = stats_from_file_metadata(
    35	        partition_values,
    36	        file_metadata,
    37	        num_indexed_cols,
    38	        stats_columns,
    39	    )?;
    40	    let stats_string = serde_json::to_string(&stats)?;
    41	
    42	    // Determine the modification timestamp to include in the add action - milliseconds since epoch
    43	    // Err should be impossible in this case since `SystemTime::now()` is always greater than `UNIX_EPOCH`
    44	    let modification_time = SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).unwrap();
    45	    let modification_time = modification_time.as_millis() as i64;
    46	
    47	    Ok(Add {
    48	        path,
    49	        size,
    50	        partition_values: partition_values
    51	            .iter()
    52	            .map(|(k, v)| {
    53	                (
    54	                    k.clone(),
    55	                    if v.is_null() {
    56	                        None
    57	                    } else {
    58	                        Some(v.serialize())
    59	                    },
    60	                )
    61	            })
    62	            .collect(),
    63	        modification_time,
    64	        data_change: true,
    65	        stats: Some(stats_string),
    66	        tags: None,
    67	        deletion_vector: None,
    68	        base_row_id: None,
    69	        default_row_commit_version: None,
    70	        clustering_provider: None,
    71	    })
    72	}
    73	
    74	// As opposed to `stats_from_file_metadata` which operates on `parquet::format::FileMetaData`,
    75	// this function produces the stats by reading the metadata from already written out files.
    76	//
    77	// Note that the file metadata used here is actually `parquet::file::metadata::FileMetaData`
    78	// which is a thrift decoding of the `parquet::format::FileMetaData` which is typically obtained
    79	// when flushing the write.
    80	pub(crate) fn stats_from_parquet_metadata(
    81	    partition_values: &IndexMap<String, Scalar>,
    82	    parquet_metadata: &ParquetMetaData,
    83	    num_indexed_cols: DataSkippingNumIndexedCols,
    84	    stats_columns: &Option<Vec<String>>,
    85	) -> Result<Stats, DeltaWriterError> {
    86	    let num_rows = parquet_metadata.file_metadata().num_rows();
    87	    let schema_descriptor = parquet_metadata.file_metadata().schema_descr_ptr();
    88	    let row_group_metadata = parquet_metadata.row_groups().to_vec();
    89	
    90	    stats_from_metadata(
    91	        partition_values,
    92	        schema_descriptor,
    93	        row_group_metadata,
    94	        num_rows,
    95	        num_indexed_cols,
    96	        stats_columns,
    97	    )
    98	}
    99	
   100	fn stats_from_file_metadata(
   101	    partition_values: &IndexMap<String, Scalar>,
   102	    file_metadata: &ParquetMetaData,
   103	    num_indexed_cols: DataSkippingNumIndexedCols,
   104	    stats_columns: &Option<Vec<impl AsRef<str>>>,
   105	) -> Result<Stats, DeltaWriterError> {
   106	    let schema_descriptor = file_metadata.file_metadata().schema_descr();
   107	
   108	    let row_group_metadata: Vec<RowGroupMetaData> = file_metadata.row_groups().to_vec();
   109	
   110	    stats_from_metadata(
   111	        partition_values,
   112	        Arc::new(schema_descriptor.clone()),
   113	        row_group_metadata,
   114	        file_metadata.file_metadata().num_rows(),
   115	        num_indexed_cols,
   116	        stats_columns,
   117	    )
   118	}
   119	
   120	fn stats_from_metadata(
   121	    partition_values: &IndexMap<String, Scalar>,
   122	    schema_descriptor: Arc<SchemaDescriptor>,
   123	    row_group_metadata: Vec<RowGroupMetaData>,
   124	    num_rows: i64,
   125	    num_indexed_cols: DataSkippingNumIndexedCols,
   126	    stats_columns: &Option<Vec<impl AsRef<str>>>,
   127	) -> Result<Stats, DeltaWriterError> {
   128	    let mut min_values: HashMap<String, ColumnValueStat> = HashMap::new();
   129	    let mut max_values: HashMap<String, ColumnValueStat> = HashMap::new();
   130	    let mut null_count: HashMap<String, ColumnCountStat> = HashMap::new();
   131	    let dialect = sqlparser::dialect::GenericDialect {};
   132	
   133	    let idx_to_iterate = if let Some(stats_cols) = stats_columns {
   134	        let stats_cols = stats_cols
   135	            .iter()
   136	            .map(|v| {
   137	                match sqlparser::parser::Parser::new(&dialect)
   138	                    .try_with_sql(v.as_ref())
   139	                    .map_err(|e| DeltaTableError::generic(e.to_string()))?
   140	                    .parse_multipart_identifier()
   141	                {
   142	                    Ok(parts) => Ok(parts.into_iter().map(|v| v.value).join(".")),
   143	                    Err(e) => Err(DeltaWriterError::DeltaTable(
   144	                        DeltaTableError::GenericError {
   145	                            source: Box::new(e),
   146	                        },
   147	                    )),
   148	                }
   149	            })
   150	            .collect::<Result<Vec<String>, DeltaWriterError>>()?;
   151	
   152	        schema_descriptor
   153	            .columns()
   154	            .iter()
   155	            .enumerate()
   156	            .filter_map(|(index, col)| {
   157	                if stats_cols.contains(&col.name().to_string()) {
   158	                    Some(index)
   159	                } else {
   160	                    None
   161	                }
   162	            })
   163	            .collect()
   164	    } else if num_indexed_cols == DataSkippingNumIndexedCols::AllColumns {
   165	        (0..schema_descriptor.num_columns()).collect::<Vec<_>>()
   166	    } else if let DataSkippingNumIndexedCols::NumColumns(n_cols) = num_indexed_cols {
   167	        (0..min(n_cols as usize, schema_descriptor.num_columns())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
   168	    } else {
   169	        return Err(DeltaWriterError::DeltaTable(DeltaTableError::Generic(
   170	            "delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols valid values are >=-1".to_string(),
   171	        )));
   172	    };
   173	
   174	    for idx in idx_to_iterate {
   175	        let column_descr = schema_descriptor.column(idx);
   176	
   177	        let column_path = column_descr.path();
   178	        let column_path_parts = column_path.parts();
   179	
   180	        // Do not include partition columns in statistics
   181	        if partition_values.contains_key(&column_path_parts[0]) {
   182	            continue;
   183	        }
   184	
   185	        let maybe_stats: Option<AggregatedStats> = row_group_metadata
   186	            .iter()
   187	            .flat_map(|g| {
   188	                g.column(idx).statistics().into_iter().filter_map(|s| {
   189	                    let is_binary = matches!(&column_descr.physical_type(), Type::BYTE_ARRAY)
   190	                        && matches!(column_descr.logical_type_ref(), Some(LogicalType::String))
   191	                            .not();
   192	                    if is_binary {
   193	                        warn!(
   194	                            "Skipping column {} because it's a binary field.",
   195	                            &column_descr.name().to_string()
   196	                        );
   197	                        None
   198	                    } else {
   199	                        Some(AggregatedStats::from((s, column_descr.logical_type_ref())))
   200	                    }
   201	                })
   202	            })
   203	            .reduce(|mut left, right| {
   204	                left += right;
   205	                left
   206	            });
   207	
   208	        if let Some(stats) = maybe_stats {
   209	            apply_min_max_for_column(
   210	                stats,
   211	                column_descr.clone(),
   212	                column_descr.path().parts(),
   213	                &mut min_values,
   214	                &mut max_values,
   215	                &mut null_count,
   216	            )?;
   217	        }
   218	    }
   219	
   220	    Ok(Stats {
   221	        min_values,
   222	        max_values,
   223	        num_records: num_rows,
   224	        null_count,
   225	    })
   226	}
   227	
   228	/// Logical scalars extracted from statistics. These are used to aggregate
   229	/// minimums and maximums. We can't use the physical scalars because they
   230	/// are not ordered correctly for some types. For example, decimals are stored
   231	/// as fixed length binary, and can't be sorted leixcographically.
   232	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, PartialOrd)]
   233	enum StatsScalar {
   234	    Boolean(bool),
   235	    Int32(i32),
   236	    Int64(i64),
   237	    Float32(f32),
   238	    Float64(f64),
   239	    Date(chrono::NaiveDate),
   240	    Timestamp(chrono::NaiveDateTime),
   241	    // We are serializing to f64 later and the ordering should be the same
   242	    // Scale is stored to handle scale=0 serialization correctly
   243	    Decimal { value: f64, scale: i32 },
   244	    String(String),
   245	    Bytes(Vec<u8>),
   246	    Uuid(uuid::Uuid),
   247	}
   248	
   249	impl StatsScalar {
   250	    fn try_from_stats(
   251	        stats: &Statistics,
   252	        logical_type: Option<&LogicalType>,
   253	        use_min: bool,
   254	    ) -> Result<Self, DeltaWriterError> {
   255	        macro_rules! get_stat {
   256	            ($val: expr) => {
   257	                if use_min {
   258	                    *$val.min_opt().unwrap()
   259	                } else {
   260	                    *$val.max_opt().unwrap()
   261	                }
   262	            };
   263	        }
   264	
   265	        match (stats, logical_type) {
   266	            (Statistics::Boolean(v), _) => Ok(Self::Boolean(get_stat!(v))),
   267	            // Int32 can be date, decimal, or just int32
   268	            (Statistics::Int32(v), Some(LogicalType::Date)) => {
   269	                let epoch_start = chrono::NaiveDate::from_ymd_opt(1970, 1, 1).unwrap(); // creating from epoch should be infallible
   270	                let date = epoch_start + chrono::Duration::days(get_stat!(v) as i64);
   271	                Ok(Self::Date(date))
   272	            }
   273	            (Statistics::Int32(v), Some(LogicalType::Decimal { scale, .. })) => {
   274	                let val = get_stat!(v) as f64 / 10.0_f64.powi(*scale);
   275	                // Spark serializes these as numbers
   276	                Ok(Self::Decimal {
   277	                    value: val,
   278	                    scale: *scale,
   279	                })
   280	            }
   281	            (Statistics::Int32(v), _) => Ok(Self::Int32(get_stat!(v))),
   282	            // Int64 can be timestamp, decimal, or integer
   283	            (Statistics::Int64(v), Some(LogicalType::Timestamp { unit, .. })) => {
   284	                // For now, we assume timestamps are adjusted to UTC. Non-UTC timestamps
   285	                // are behind a feature gate in Delta:
   286	                // https://github.com/delta-io/delta/blob/master/PROTOCOL.md#timestamp-without-timezone-timestampntz
   287	                let v = get_stat!(v);
   288	                let timestamp = match unit {
   289	                    TimeUnit::MILLIS => chrono::DateTime::from_timestamp_millis(v),
   290	                    TimeUnit::MICROS => chrono::DateTime::from_timestamp_micros(v),
   291	                    TimeUnit::NANOS => {
   292	                        let secs = v / 1_000_000_000;
   293	                        let nanosecs = (v % 1_000_000_000) as u32;
   294	                        chrono::DateTime::from_timestamp(secs, nanosecs)
   295	                    }
   296	                };
   297	                let timestamp = timestamp.ok_or(DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   298	                    debug_value: v.to_string(),
   299	                    logical_type: logical_type.cloned(),
   300	                })?;
   301	                Ok(Self::Timestamp(timestamp.naive_utc()))
   302	            }
   303	            (Statistics::Int64(v), Some(LogicalType::Decimal { scale, .. })) => {
   304	                let val = get_stat!(v) as f64 / 10.0_f64.powi(*scale);
   305	                // Spark serializes these as numbers
   306	                Ok(Self::Decimal {
   307	                    value: val,
   308	                    scale: *scale,
   309	                })
   310	            }
   311	            (Statistics::Int64(v), _) => Ok(Self::Int64(get_stat!(v))),
   312	            (Statistics::Float(v), _) => Ok(Self::Float32(get_stat!(v))),
   313	            (Statistics::Double(v), _) => Ok(Self::Float64(get_stat!(v))),
   314	            (Statistics::ByteArray(v), logical_type) => {
   315	                let bytes = if use_min {
   316	                    v.min_bytes_opt()
   317	                } else {
   318	                    v.max_bytes_opt()
   319	                }
   320	                .unwrap_or_default();
   321	                match logical_type {
   322	                    None => Ok(Self::Bytes(bytes.to_vec())),
   323	                    Some(LogicalType::String) => {
   324	                        Ok(Self::String(String::from_utf8(bytes.to_vec()).map_err(
   325	                            |_| DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   326	                                debug_value: format!("{bytes:?}"),
   327	                                logical_type: Some(LogicalType::String),
   328	                            },
   329	                        )?))
   330	                    }
   331	                    _ => Err(DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   332	                        debug_value: format!("{bytes:?}"),
   333	                        logical_type: logical_type.cloned(),
   334	                    }),
   335	                }
   336	            }
   337	            (Statistics::FixedLenByteArray(v), Some(LogicalType::Decimal { scale, precision })) => {
   338	                let val = if use_min {
   339	                    v.min_bytes_opt()
   340	                } else {
   341	                    v.max_bytes_opt()
   342	                }
   343	                .unwrap_or_default();
   344	
   345	                let val = if val.len() <= 16 {
   346	                    i128::from_be_bytes(sign_extend_be(val)) as f64
   347	                } else {
   348	                    return Err(DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   349	                        debug_value: format!("{val:?}"),
   350	                        logical_type: Some(LogicalType::Decimal {
   351	                            scale: *scale,
   352	                            precision: *precision,
   353	                        }),
   354	                    });
   355	                };
   356	
   357	                let mut val = val / 10.0_f64.powi(*scale);
   358	
   359	                if val.is_normal()
   360	                    && (val.trunc() as i128).to_string().len() > (precision - scale) as usize
   361	                {
   362	                    // For normal values with integer parts that get rounded to a number beyond
   363	                    // the precision - scale range take the next smaller (by magnitude) value
   364	                    val = f64::from_bits(val.to_bits() - 1);
   365	                }
   366	
   367	                Ok(Self::Decimal {
   368	                    value: val,
   369	                    scale: *scale,
   370	                })
   371	            }
   372	            (Statistics::FixedLenByteArray(v), Some(LogicalType::Uuid)) => {
   373	                let val = if use_min {
   374	                    v.min_bytes_opt()
   375	                } else {
   376	                    v.max_bytes_opt()
   377	                }
   378	                .unwrap_or_default();
   379	
   380	                if val.len() != 16 {
   381	                    return Err(DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   382	                        debug_value: format!("{val:?}"),
   383	                        logical_type: Some(LogicalType::Uuid),
   384	                    });
   385	                }
   386	
   387	                let mut bytes = [0; 16];
   388	                bytes.copy_from_slice(val);
   389	
   390	                let val = uuid::Uuid::from_bytes(bytes);
   391	                Ok(Self::Uuid(val))
   392	            }
   393	            (stats, _) => Err(DeltaWriterError::StatsParsingFailed {
   394	                debug_value: format!("{stats:?}"),
   395	                logical_type: logical_type.cloned(),
   396	            }),
   397	        }
   398	    }
   399	}
   400	
   401	/// Performs big endian sign extension
   402	/// Copied from arrow-rs repo/parquet crate:
   403	/// https://github.com/apache/arrow-rs/blob/b25c441745602c9967b1e3cc4a28bc469cfb1311/parquet/src/arrow/buffer/bit_util.rs#L54
   404	pub fn sign_extend_be<const N: usize>(b: &[u8]) -> [u8; N] {
   405	    assert!(b.len() <= N, "Array too large, expected less than {N}");
   406	    let is_negative = (b[0] & 128u8) == 128u8;
   407	    let mut result = if is_negative { [255u8; N] } else { [0u8; N] };
   408	    for (d, s) in result.iter_mut().skip(N - b.len()).zip(b) {
   409	        *d = *s;
   410	    }
   411	    result
   412	}
   413	
   414	impl From<StatsScalar> for serde_json::Value {
   415	    fn from(scalar: StatsScalar) -> Self {
   416	        match scalar {
   417	            StatsScalar::Boolean(v) => serde_json::Value::Bool(v),
   418	            StatsScalar::Int32(v) => serde<response clipped><NOTE>To save on context only part of this file has been shown to you. You should retry this tool after you have searched inside the file with `grep -n` in order to find the line numbers of what you are looking for.</NOTE>

   424	
   425	    fn create_writer(
   426	        object_store: ObjectStoreRef,
   427	        path: Path,
   428	        config: &PartitionWriterConfig,
   429	    ) -> DeltaResult<LazyArrowWriter> {
   430	        let state = LazyArrowWriter::Initialized(path, object_store.clone(), config.clone());
   431	        Ok(state)
   432	    }
   433	
   434	    fn next_data_path(&mut self) -> Path {
   435	        self.part_counter += 1;
   436	
   437	        next_data_path(
   438	            &self.config.prefix,
   439	            self.part_counter,
   440	            &self.writer_id,
   441	            &self.config.writer_properties,
   442	        )
   443	    }
   444	
   445	    fn reset_writer(&mut self) -> DeltaResult<()> {
   446	        let next_path = self.next_data_path();
   447	        let new_writer = Self::create_writer(self.object_store.clone(), next_path, &self.config)?;
   448	        let state = std::mem::replace(&mut self.writer, new_writer);
   449	
   450	        if let LazyArrowWriter::Writing(path, arrow_writer) = state {
   451	            self.in_flight_writers
   452	                .spawn(upload_parquet_file(arrow_writer, path));
   453	        }
   454	        Ok(())
   455	    }
   456	
   457	    /// Buffers record batches in-memory up to appx. `target_file_size`.
   458	    /// Flushes data to storage once a full file can be written.
   459	    ///
   460	    /// The `close` method has to be invoked to write all data still buffered
   461	    /// and get the list of all written files.
   462	    pub async fn write(&mut self, batch: &RecordBatch) -> DeltaResult<()> {
   463	        if batch.schema() != self.config.file_schema {
   464	            return Err(WriteError::SchemaMismatch {
   465	                schema: batch.schema(),
   466	                expected_schema: self.config.file_schema.clone(),
   467	            }
   468	            .into());
   469	        }
   470	
   471	        let max_offset = batch.num_rows();
   472	        for offset in (0..max_offset).step_by(self.config.write_batch_size) {
   473	            let length = usize::min(self.config.write_batch_size, max_offset - offset);
   474	            self.writer
   475	                .write_batch(&batch.slice(offset, length))
   476	                .await?;
   477	            if let Some(target_file_size) = self.config.target_file_size {
   478	                let estimated_size = self.writer.estimated_size();
   479	                // flush currently buffered data to disk once we meet or exceed the target file size.
   480	                if estimated_size as u64 >= target_file_size.get() {
   481	                    debug!("Writing file with estimated size {estimated_size:?} in background.");
   482	                    self.reset_writer()?;
   483	                }
   484	            }
   485	        }
   486	
   487	        Ok(())
   488	    }
   489	
   490	    /// Close the writer and get the new [Add] actions.
   491	    ///
   492	    /// This will flush any remaining data and collect all Add actions from background tasks.
   493	    pub async fn close(mut self) -> DeltaResult<Vec<Add>> {
   494	        if let LazyArrowWriter::Writing(path, arrow_writer) = self.writer {
   495	            self.in_flight_writers
   496	                .spawn(upload_parquet_file(arrow_writer, path));
   497	        }
   498	
   499	        let mut results = Vec::new();
   500	        while let Some(result) = self.in_flight_writers.join_next().await {

    25	pub(crate) fn next_data_path(
    26	    prefix: &Path,
    27	    part_count: usize,
    28	    writer_id: &Uuid,
    29	    writer_properties: &WriterProperties,
    30	) -> Path {
    31	    fn compression_to_str(compression: &Compression) -> &str {
    32	        match compression {
    33	            // This is to match HADOOP's convention
    34	            // https://github.com/apache/parquet-mr/blob/c4977579ab3b149ea045a177b039f055b5408e8f/parquet-common/src/main/java/org/apache/parquet/hadoop/metadata/CompressionCodecName.java#L27-L34
    35	            Compression::UNCOMPRESSED => "",
    36	            Compression::SNAPPY => ".snappy",
    37	            Compression::GZIP(_) => ".gz",
    38	            Compression::LZO => ".lzo",
    39	            Compression::BROTLI(_) => ".br",
    40	            Compression::LZ4 => ".lz4",
    41	            Compression::ZSTD(_) => ".zstd",
    42	            Compression::LZ4_RAW => ".lz4raw",
    43	        }
    44	    }
    45	
    46	    // We can not access the default column properties but the current implementation will return
    47	    // the default compression when the column is not found
    48	    let column_path = ColumnPath::new(Vec::new());
    49	    let compression = writer_properties.compression(&column_path);
    50	
    51	    let part = format!("{part_count:0>5}");
    52	
    53	    // TODO: what does c000 mean?
    54	    let file_name = format!(
    55	        "part-{part}-{writer_id}-c000{}.parquet",
    56	        compression_to_str(&compression)
    57	    );
    58	    prefix.child(file_name)
    59	}
    60	
    61	/// Convert a vector of json values to a RecordBatch
    62	pub fn record_batch_from_message(
    63	    arrow_schema: Arc<ArrowSchema>,
    64	    json: &[Value],
    65	) -> DeltaResult<RecordBatch> {
    66	    let mut decoder = ReaderBuilder::new(arrow_schema).build_decoder().unwrap();
    67	    decoder.serialize(json)?;
    68	    decoder
    69	        .flush()?
    70	        .ok_or_else(|| DeltaWriterError::EmptyRecordBatch.into())

    55	/// Auxiliary methods for dealing with kernel scalars
    56	pub trait ScalarExt: Sized {
    57	    /// Serialize to string
    58	    fn serialize(&self) -> String;
    59	    /// Serialize to string for use in hive partition file names
    60	    fn serialize_encoded(&self) -> String;
    61	    /// Create a [`Scalar`] from an arrow array row
    62	    fn from_array(arr: &dyn Array, index: usize) -> Option<Self>;
    63	    /// Serialize as serde_json::Value
    64	    fn to_json(&self) -> Value;
    65	}
    66	
    67	impl ScalarExt for Scalar {
    68	    /// Serializes this scalar as a string.
    69	    fn serialize(&self) -> String {
    70	        match self {
    71	            Self::String(s) => s.to_owned(),
    72	            Self::Byte(b) => b.to_string(),
    73	            Self::Short(s) => s.to_string(),
    74	            Self::Integer(i) => i.to_string(),
    75	            Self::Long(l) => l.to_string(),
    76	            Self::Float(f) => f.to_string(),
    77	            Self::Double(d) => d.to_string(),
    78	            Self::Boolean(b) => if *b { "true" } else { "false" }.to_string(),
    79	            Self::TimestampNtz(ts) | Self::Timestamp(ts) => {
    80	                let ts = Utc.timestamp_micros(*ts).single().unwrap();
    81	                ts.format("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S%.6f").to_string()
    82	            }
    83	            Self::Date(days) => {
    84	                let date = DateTime::from_timestamp(*days as i64 * 24 * 3600, 0).unwrap();
    85	                date.format("%Y-%m-%d").to_string()
    86	            }
    87	            Self::Decimal(decimal) => match decimal.scale().cmp(&0) {
    88	                Ordering::Equal => decimal.bits().to_string(),
    89	                Ordering::Greater => {
    90	                    let scale = decimal.scale();
    91	                    let value = decimal.bits();
    92	                    let scalar_multiple = 10_i128.pow(scale as u32);
    93	                    let mut s = String::new();
    94	                    s.push_str((value / scalar_multiple).to_string().as_str());
    95	                    s.push('.');
    96	                    s.push_str(&format!(
    97	                        "{:0>scale$}",
    98	                        value % scalar_multiple,
    99	                        scale = scale as usize
   100	                    ));
   101	                    s
   102	                }
   103	                Ordering::Less => {
   104	                    let mut s = decimal.bits().to_string();
   105	                    for _ in 0..decimal.scale() {
   106	                        s.push('0');
   107	                    }
   108	                    s
   109	                }
   110	            },
   111	            Self::Binary(val) => create_escaped_binary_string(val.as_slice()),
   112	            Self::Null(_) => "null".to_string(),
   113	            Self::Struct(_) => self.to_string(),
   114	            Self::Array(_) => self.to_string(),
   115	            Self::Map(_) => self.to_string(),
   116	        }
   117	    }
   118	
   119	    /// Serializes this scalar as a string for use in hive partition file names.
   120	    fn serialize_encoded(&self) -> String {
   121	        if self.is_null() {
   122	            return NULL_PARTITION_VALUE_DATA_PATH.to_string();
   123	        }
   124	        encode_partition_value(self.serialize().as_str())
   125	    }
   126	
   127	    /// Create a [`Scalar`] from a row in an arrow array.
   128	    fn from_array(arr: &dyn Array, index: usize) -> Option<Self> {
   129	        use arrow_array::*;
   130	        use arrow_schema::DataType::*;
   131	
   132	        if arr.len() <= index {
   133	            return None;
   134	        }
   135	        if arr.is_null(index) {
   136	            return Some(Self::Null(arr.data_type().try_into_kernel().ok()?));
   137	        }
   138	
   139	        match arr.data_type() {
   140	            Utf8 => arr
   141	                .as_any()
   142	                .downcast_ref::<StringArray>()
   143	                .map(|v| checked(v, index, Self::String(v.value(index).to_string()))),
   144	            LargeUtf8 => arr
   145	                .as_any()

    49	fn encode_partition_value(value: &str) -> String {
    50	    utf8_percent_encode(value, RFC3986_PART).to_string()
    51	}

2026-05-31 12:42:10,416 - INFO - swea-agent-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - ========================= STEP 51 =========================
2026-05-31 12:42:10,424 - DEBUG - swea-lm-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - n_cache_control: 2
2026-05-31 12:42:37,033 - DEBUG - swea-lm-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - Response: ModelResponse(id='6abe087c5dec4f63881f603d68492387', created=1780231332, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content='So `encode_partition_value` uses `utf8_percent_encode` which percent-encodes special characters including spaces as `%20`.\n\nSo the path written to the Add action would be something like:\n`group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`\n\nNow, when `LogicalFileView::path()` is called, it does:\n```rust\npub fn path(&self) -> Cow<\'_, str> {\n    let raw = get_string_value(...);\n    percent_decode_str(raw).decode_utf8_lossy()\n}\n```\n\nSo `path()` returns `group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet` (decoded).\nAnd `path_raw()` returns `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet` (raw).\n\nNow in the merge code:\n```rust\nfor action in snapshot.log_data() {\n    let rel_path = action.path();  // percent-decoded: "group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"\n    let rel_path_str = rel_path.as_ref();  // "group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"\n    if should_remove_rewritten_file(&survivors, rel_path_str, &table_root)? {\n```\n\nAnd `should_remove_rewritten_file`:\n1. `survivors.contains(rel_path)` - survivors contain full URLs like `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`. This won\'t match the decoded relative path.\n2. `normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, ...)` - This takes `rel_path` = `"group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"` and joins it with `table_root` (`file:///path/to/table/`). 2026-05-31 12:42:37,040 - INFO - swea-agent-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - 💭 THOUGHT
So `encode_partition_value` uses `utf8_percent_encode` which percent-encodes special characters including spaces as `%20`.

So the path written to the Add action would be something like:
`group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`

Now, when `LogicalFileView::path()` is called, it does:
```rust
pub fn path(&self) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    let raw = get_string_value(...);
    percent_decode_str(raw).decode_utf8_lossy()
}
```

So `path()` returns `group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet` (decoded).
And `path_raw()` returns `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet` (raw).

Now in the merge code:
```rust
for action in snapshot.log_data() {
    let rel_path = action.path();  // percent-decoded: "group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"
    let rel_path_str = rel_path.as_ref();  // "group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"
    if should_remove_rewritten_file(&survivors, rel_path_str, &table_root)? {
```

And `should_remove_rewritten_file`:
1. `survivors.contains(rel_path)` - survivors contain full URLs like `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`. This won't match the decoded relative path.
2. `normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, ...)` - This takes `rel_path` = `"group=foo bar/part-00000-...parquet"` and joins it with `table_root` (`file:///path/to/table/`). The `url.join(path)` will encode the space as `%20`, resulting in `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`.

Now in the survivors, the file URLs were created from `f.file_url.as_str()` which is from `parse_path(table_root, view.path_raw())` where `path_raw()` returns `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`. Joining with table root gives `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet`.

So both should produce the same URL. Let me verify this more carefully by writing a reproduction script.

Actually, wait - let me re-examine `parse_path` more carefully:

```rust
fn parse_path(url: &Url, path: &str) -> DeltaResult<Url, DataFusionError> {
    Ok(match Url::parse(path) {
        Ok(url) => url,
        Err(_) => url.join(path).map_err(|e| DataFusionError::External(Box::new(e)))?,
    })
}
```

If `path` = `"group=foo%20bar/part-00000-...parquet"`, `Url::parse` would fail (since it's a relative path), and `url.join(path)` would be called. But `url.join(path)` when `path` already contains `%20` - the `%` is a special character in URLs. Let me think...

Actually, `url.join(path)` takes a string and parses it as a relative URL reference, then resolves it against the base URL. If the input is `group=foo%20bar/...`, the `%20` would be kept as `%20` since it's already percent-encoded. If the input is `group=foo bar/...`, the space would be encoded as `%20`.

So both paths should result in the same URL. That means the comparison should work...

But wait, maybe the issue is different. Let me look at how the survivors set is populated more carefully. The survivors are populated in `MergeBarrierStream::Finalize`:

```rust
State::Finalize => {
    for part in &mut self.file_partitions {
        if let Some(batch) = part.drain() {
            return Poll::Ready(Some(Ok(batch)));
        }
    }
    {
        for part in &self.file_partitions {
            match part.state {
                PartitionBarrierState::Closed => {}
                PartitionBarrierState::Open => {
                    if let Some(file_name) = &part.file_name {
                        self.survivors.insert(file_name.to_owned());
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    self.state = State::Done;
    continue;
}
```

And `file_name` is populated from `get_path_column`:

```rust
State::Feed => {
    match self.input.poll_next_unpin(cx) {
        Poll::Ready(Some(Ok(batch))) => {
            let file_dictionary = get_path_column(&batch, &self.file_column)?;
            // ...
            for file_name in file_dictionary.values().into_iter() {
                let key = match file_name {
                    Some(name) => {
                        if !self.map.contains_key(name) {
                            let key = self.file_partitions.len();
                            let part_stream = MergeBarrierPartition::new(Some(name.to_string()));
                            self.file_partitions.push(part_stream);
                            self.map.insert(name.to_string(), key);
                        }
                        *self.map.get(name).unwrap()
                    }
                    None => 0,
                };
                key_map.push(key)
            }
```

So `file_name` comes from the dictionary values of the path column in the batch. This path column is the file ID column added during the scan.

Now, how is the file ID column populated? Let me trace back to the scan execution.

In `scan/mod.rs` line 326:
```rust
let file_value = wrap_file_id_value(f.file_url.as_str());
// ...
partitioned_file.partition_values = vec![file_value.clone()];
```

So the file ID partition value is `f.file_url.as_str()`. The `file_url` is a `Url` object. `Url::as_str()` returns the URL string representation.

Now, `f.file_url` was constructed in `extract_file_statistics`:
```rust
parse_path(scan.snapshot().table_root(), view.path_raw()).ok()?
```

Where `path_raw()` returns the raw (not percent-decoded) path. So the URL should contain the percent-encoded version of the path.

Now later, in the merge code, `should_remove_rewritten_file` is called with `rel_path` from `action.path()` which is percent-decoded. Then `normalize_path_as_file_id` constructs a full URL.

Let me actually write a test script to reproduce the issue and debug it:







🎬 ACTION
cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/reproduce.py << 'EOF'
import os
import sys
import tempfile
import pyarrow as pa

# Add the python bindings to path
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(os.path.dirname("/testbed"), "python"))

from deltalake import DeltaTable

_TS = 1_704_067_200_000_000  # 2024-01-01 00:00:00 UTC in microseconds

def make_data(group):
    n = 3
    return pa.table({
        "group": pa.array([group] * n, type=pa.string()),
        "region": pa.array(["A", "B", "C"], type=pa.string()),
        "ts": pa.array([_TS] * n, type=pa.timestamp("us")),
        "val": pa.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], type=pa.float64()),
    })

def run(group, path):
    data = make_data(group)
    predicate = f"target.group = '{group}' AND target.region = source.region AND target.ts = source.ts"
    DeltaTable.create(table_uri=path, schema=data.schema, partition_by=["group"])
    dt = DeltaTable(path)
    for i in range(1, 3):
        dt.merge(source=data, predicate=predicate, source_alias="source", target_alias="target") \
          .when_matched_update_all().when_not_matched_insert_all().execute()
        dt.update_incremental()
        metrics = dt.history(1)[0]["operationMetrics"]
        print(f"  merge {i}: rows={dt.to_pandas().shape[0]}, files_removed={metrics.get('num_target_files_removed')}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:
        print("With space:")
        run("foo bar", os.path.join(tmp, "a"))
        print("Without space:")
        run("foo_bar", os.path.join(tmp, "b"))
EOF

2026-05-31 12:43:12,972 - DEBUG - swea-lm-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - Response: ModelResponse(id='5b9d33e96201492a88aa4b494563518a', created=1780231367, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content='The Python environment doesn\'t have the required packages. Let me focus on the Rust code directly to understand the issue.\n\nLet me look at the key comparison more carefully. In `should_remove_rewritten_file`:\n\n```rust\nfn should_remove_rewritten_file(\n    survivors: &barrier::BarrierSurvivorSet,\n    rel_path: &str,\n    table_root: &url::Url,\n) -> DeltaResult<bool> {\n    if survivors.contains(rel_path) {\n        return Ok(true);\n    }\n    // Compare against normalized file IDs.\n    let full_id = normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, "merge remove")?;\n    Ok(survivors.contains(full_id.as_str()))\n}\n```\n\nThe `survivors` set contains strings that were inserted from the batch\'s file column. These come from `get_path_column` which returns the values from the dictionary encoded `__delta_rs_path` column.\n\nNow, the `__delta_rs_path` column is populated from `file_value = wrap_file_id_value(f.file_url.as_str())`.\n\nBut wait - there\'s ALSO the case where the scan is metadata-only (`DeltaScanMetaExec`). In that case, the file ID is populated differently. Let me look at the key comparison more carefully. In `should_remove_rewritten_file`:

```rust
fn should_remove_rewritten_file(
    survivors: &barrier::BarrierSurvivorSet,
    rel_path: &str,
    table_root: &url::Url,
) -> DeltaResult<bool> {
    if survivors.contains(rel_path) {
        return Ok(true);
    }
    // Compare against normalized file IDs.
    let full_id = normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, "merge remove")?;
    Ok(survivors.contains(full_id.as_str()))
}
```

The `survivors` set contains strings that were inserted from the batch's file column. These come from `get_path_column` which returns the values from the dictionary encoded `__delta_rs_path` column.

```rust
if let Some(file_id_field) = &self.file_id_field {
    let row_count = result.num_rows();
    let keys = UInt16Array::from(vec![0u16; row_count]);
    let values: ArrayRef = match file_id_field.data_type() {
        DataType::Dictionary(_, value_type)
            if value_type.as_ref() == &DataType::Utf8View =>
        {
            if row_count == 0 {
                Arc::new(StringViewArray::from_iter_values(std::iter::empty::<&str>()))
            } else {
                Arc::new(StringViewArray::from_iter_values([file_id.as_str()]))
            }
        }
        _ => {
            if row_count == 0 {
                Arc::new(StringArray::from(Vec::<Option<&str>>::new()))
            } else {
                Arc::new(StringArray::from(vec![Some(file_id.as_str())]))
            }
        }
    };
    let file_id_array: DictionaryArray<UInt16Type> =
        DictionaryArray::try_new(keys, values)?;
    ...
```

So for metadata-only scan, the file_id is `file_id.as_str()` which comes from `ScanFileContext.file_url.to_string()` (line 92). 

For the regular data scan, the file_id comes from `partitioned_file.partition_values = vec![file_value.clone()]` where `file_value = wrap_file_id_value(f.file_url.as_str())`. DataFusion then uses this as a partition value and puts it in the `__delta_rs_path` column.

Now, is there a difference between how the metadata-only scan and data scan handle the file IDs? Let me check if the merge uses metadata-only scan or data scan.

In the merge, the target scan is created via `DeltaScanNext::builder()`. The type of scan depends on the projection and predicates. Let me check if there could be a difference.

Actually, let me focus on the actual bug. Let me look more carefully at `normalize_path_as_file_id_with_table_root`:

```rust
fn normalize_path_as_file_id_with_table_root(
    path: &str,
    table_root_url: &Url,
    context: &'static str,
) -> crate::DeltaResult<String> {
    debug_assert!(
        table_root_url.path().ends_with('/'),
        "table_root_url must end with '/' before normalization"
    );

    let file_url = Url::parse(path)
        .or_else(|_| table_root_url.join(path))
        .map_err(|e| {
            DeltaTableError::InvalidTableLocation(format!(
                "Failed to normalize {context} '{}' against '{}': {e}",
                redact_url_str_for_error(path),
                redact_url_for_error(table_root_url),
            ))
        })?;

    Ok(file_url.to_string())
}
```

If `path` is something like `"group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet"` (with a literal space, because it was percent-decoded), then:
1. `Url::parse("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet")` - this will FAIL because spaces are not valid in URLs.
2. `table_root_url.join("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet")` - this will percent-encode the space to `%20`, resulting in `file:///path/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Now, the survivors contain URLs from `f.file_url.as_str()`. The `file_url` was built from `parse_path(table_root, view.path_raw())`:
```rust
fn parse_path(url: &Url, path: &str) -> DeltaResult<Url, DataFusionError> {
    Ok(match Url::parse(path) {
        Ok(url) => url,
        Err(_) => url.join(path).map_err(|e| DataFusionError::External(Box::new(e)))?,
    })
}
```

If `path_raw()` returns `"group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet"` (already percent-encoded):
1. `Url::parse("group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet")` - this FAILS because it's a relative path.
2. `url.join("group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet")` - this preserves the `%20` as is, resulting in `file:///path/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

So both should result in the same URL! But wait, is `path_raw()` actually returning percent-encoded paths?

Let me check how the path is stored in the Add action and how it comes to the kernel. The path is stored in the JSON log as part of the Add action. It's stored as-is. When Delta Kernel reads the log, it parses the JSON and stores the path string.

The key question: does the path stored in the Add action contain `%20` or a literal space?

From the writer code:
- `partition_values.hive_partition_path()` produces e.g., `"group=foo%20bar"` (using `serialize_encoded` which percent-encodes)
- `next_data_path` produces `prefix.child(file_name)` where `prefix` is `Path::parse("group=foo%20bar")` and file_name is like `"part-00000-xxx.parquet"`
- The resulting path is `"group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet"`

So the path stored in Add should have `%20`. But wait, `Path::parse` might interpret `%20` differently. Let me check what `Path::parse` does with percent-encoded strings.

In the object_store crate, `Path::parse` parses a string as a path. But actually, `Path::parse` might not decode percent-encoding - it might just treat it as literal characters.

Actually, looking at the code in `crates/core/src/kernel/snapshot/iterators.rs` line 134-139:

```rust
pub(crate) fn object_store_path(&self) -> Path {
    let path = self.path();  // percent-decoded!
    // Try to preserve percent encoding if possible
    match Path::parse(path.as_ref()) {
        Ok(path) => path,
        Err(_) => Path::from(path.as_ref()),
    }
}
```

This comment is interesting: "Try to preserve percent encoding if possible". It uses `self.path()` which is percent-decoded, then tries to parse it. If it has spaces (after decoding), `Path::parse` would fail, and it falls back to `Path::from`.

Now, `Path::from` just wraps the string as-is. And `Path::parse` would also just use the string as-is (it doesn't decode percent encoding either).

`action.path()` returns `LogicalFileView::path()` which is percent-decoded.

Now, `should_remove_rewritten_file` first checks `survivors.contains(rel_path_str)`. The survivors contain strings from the file column values. Those come from `f.file_url.as_str()`. 

Let me check what `f.file_url` looks like for a local file. The table root is something like `file:///path/to/table/`. The `path_raw()` returns something like `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`. `parse_path` calls `url.join(path)` which resolves the relative path against the base URL, giving `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Then `f.file_url.as_str()` returns `"file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet"`.

So `survivors.contains("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet")` (the decoded relative path) would return false.

Then `normalize_path_as_file_id("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet", table_root, ...)` is called. This calls `table_root.join("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet")` which should produce `file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`. Then `survivors.contains("file:///path/to/table/group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet")` should return true.

So theoretically it should work. But maybe there's a subtle difference...

Let me check if `url.join` might produce a different encoding. Let me look at the `url` crate's behavior:

Actually, I think the issue might be with how `Path::parse` works in `object_store`. Let me check the `object_store` crate's Path implementation.

Actually, let me check something else. Let me look at `ensure_table_root_url`:

   152	pub(crate) fn ensure_table_root_url(table_root_url: &Url) -> Url {
   153	    if table_root_url.path().ends_with('/') {
   154	        table_root_url.clone()
   155	    } else {
   156	        let mut table_root_url = table_root_url.clone();
   157	        table_root_url.set_path(&format!("{}/", table_root_url.path()));
   158	        table_root_url
   159	    }
   160	}
   161	
   162	pub(crate) fn redact_url_for_error(url: &Url) -> String {
   163	    let mut redacted = url.clone();
   164	    let _ = redacted.set_password(None);
   165	    let _ = redacted.set_username("");
   166	    redacted.set_query(None);
   167	    redacted.set_fragment(None);
   168	    redacted.to_string()
   169	}
   170	
   171	pub(crate) fn redact_url_str_for_error(urlish: &str) -> String {
   172	    Url::parse(urlish).map_or_else(
   173	        |_| {
   174	            urlish
   175	                .split(['?', '#'])
   176	                .next()
   177	                .unwrap_or(urlish)
   178	                .to_string()
   179	        },
   180	        |url| redact_url_for_error(&url),
   181	    )
   182	}

    97	pub(crate) struct FileSelection {
    98	    #[serde(with = "serde_file_id_set")]
    99	    file_ids: HashSet<String>,
   100	    missing_file_policy: MissingFilePolicy,
   101	}
   102	
   103	impl FileSelection {
   104	    /// Create a selection from pre-normalized file IDs.
   105	    ///
   106	    /// This constructor does not normalize or validate the input IDs.
   107	    /// Callers with relative file paths or Add actions should prefer:
   108	    /// - [`FileSelection::from_file_paths`]
   109	    /// - [`FileSelection::from_adds`]
   110	    /// - [`normalize_path_as_file_id`]
   111	    pub(crate) fn new(file_ids: impl IntoIterator<Item = String>) -> Self {
   112	        Self {
   113	            file_ids: file_ids.into_iter().collect(),
   114	            missing_file_policy: MissingFilePolicy::Error,
   115	        }
   116	    }
   117	
   118	    pub(crate) fn with_missing_file_policy(mut self, policy: MissingFilePolicy) -> Self {
   119	        self.missing_file_policy = policy;
   120	        self
   121	    }
   122	
   123	    pub(crate) fn from_file_paths(
   124	        file_paths: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl AsRef<str>>,
   125	        table_root_url: &Url,
   126	    ) -> crate::DeltaResult<Self> {
   127	        let table_root_url = ensure_table_root_url(table_root_url);
   128	        let mut file_ids = HashSet::new();
   129	        for path in file_paths {
   130	            let file_id = normalize_path_as_file_id_with_table_root(
   131	                path.as_ref(),
   132	                &table_root_url,
   133	                "file path",
   134	            )?;
   135	            file_ids.insert(file_id);
   136	        }
   137	
   138	        Ok(Self {
   139	            file_ids,
   140	            missing_file_policy: MissingFilePolicy::Error,
   141	        })
   142	    }
   143	
   144	    pub(crate) fn from_adds(
   145	        adds: impl IntoIterator<Item = crate::kernel::Add>,
   146	        table_root_url: &Url,
   147	    ) -> crate::DeltaResult<Self> {
   148	        Self::from_file_paths(adds.into_iter().map(|a| a.path), table_root_url)
   149	    }
   150	}

  1174	    fn encode_path(path: &str) -> String {
  1175	        percent_encode(path.as_bytes(), INVALID).to_string()
  1176	    }

2026-05-31 12:45:31,149 - INFO - swea-agent-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I found something very important!

The `serde_path` module:
- **serialize**: Encodes the path using `percent_encode` with the `INVALID` set. The `INVALID` set includes `%` (line 1157: `.add(b'%')`), but does NOT include space. So when serializing, spaces are NOT percent-encoded. But `%` IS encoded.

- **deserialize**: Decodes the path using `percent_decode_str`.

So when an Add action is written to the JSON log:
1. The path `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet` is encoded: `%` becomes `%25`, so the stored path becomes `group=foo%2520bar/part-00000.parquet`. Wait no, that's not right. Let me re-read.

The `encode_path` function encodes using `INVALID` set. The `INVALID` set includes `%` (and other characters like `\`, `{`, etc.). So `%` is encoded as `%25`. But space is NOT in the `INVALID` set, so space is NOT encoded.

But wait, the path stored in Add is created from the writer. The writer uses `Path::parse(partition_values.hive_partition_path())` where `hive_partition_path()` uses `v.serialize_encoded()` which percent-encodes spaces as `%20`. So the path is `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

When this path is serialized to JSON via `serde_path::serialize`, `%` becomes `%25`, so the stored path becomes `group=foo%2520bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Then when it's deserialized via `serde_path::deserialize`, `%25` becomes `%` again, giving `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

So in the Add struct, `path` should be `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet` (percent-encoded space).

Now, `LogicalFileView::path()` (used in `LogDataHandler` iteration) calls `percent_decode_str(raw).decode_utf8_lossy()`. This would decode `%20` to a space, giving `group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet`.

And `LogicalFileView::path_raw()` returns the raw string without decoding, which would be `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Now, in the merge code:
- `action.path()` returns the decoded path: `group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet`
- `should_remove_rewritten_file` is called with this decoded path
- `normalize_path_as_file_id(rel_path, table_root, ...)` joins this decoded path with the table root

But `url.join("group=foo bar/part-00000.parquet")` will encode the space as `%20`, resulting in `file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Meanwhile, in the scan, `ScanFileContext.file_url` is built from `parse_path(table_root, view.path_raw())` where `path_raw()` returns the raw (non-decoded) path: `group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`. Then `url.join("group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet")` keeps `%20` as-is, resulting in `file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000.parquet`.

Both should match! So `survivors.contains(full_id.as_str())` should return true.

    35	    fn parse_path(path: &str) -> Path {
    36	        // Path::from will percent-encode the input, while Path::parse won't. So
    37	        // we should prefer Path::parse.
    38	        match Path::parse(path) {
    39	            Ok(path) => path,
    40	            Err(_) => Path::from(path),

2026-05-31 12:46:10,366 - INFO - swea-agent-delta-io__delta-rs-4357 - 💭 THOUGHT
Interesting! The comment says "Path::from will percent-encode the input, while Path::parse won't."

So there are two ways to create a Path:
- `Path::parse` - does NOT percent-encode
- `Path::from` - DOES percent-encode

Now let me check how the writer creates the path. In `writer.rs` line 226:
```rust
let partition_key = Path::parse(partition_values.hive_partition_path())?;
```

And line 324:
```rust
let prefix = Path::parse(part_path)?;
```

Where `part_path = partition_values.hive_partition_path()` which returns e.g., `"group=foo%20bar"` (already percent-encoded). `Path::parse` does NOT percent-encode, so it keeps `%20` as literal characters.

Then in `next_data_path`:
```rust
prefix.child(file_name)
```

This produces a path like `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`.

So the path stored in the Add action (via `path.to_string()`) should be `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet` (with literal `%20`).

Now, when this path is serialized to JSON (via `serde_path::serialize`), the `%` character is in the `INVALID` set, so it gets encoded as `%25`. So in the JSON file, the path is `group=foo%2520bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`.

When deserialized (via `serde_path::deserialize`), `%25` is decoded back to `%`, giving `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`.

Now, `LogicalFileView::path_raw()` returns this raw path: `group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`.
And `LogicalFileView::path()` decodes it to: `group=foo bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`.

Now in the merge:
- `action.path()` → `"group=foo bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet"` (decoded)
- `should_remove_rewritten_file`:
  - `survivors.contains("group=foo bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet")` → false
  - `normalize_path_as_file_id("group=foo bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet", table_root, ...)`:
    - `table_root.join("group=foo bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet")` → percent-encodes space → `file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`
  - `survivors.contains("file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet")` → should be true

Now in the scan:
- `f.file_url` = `parse_path(table_root, view.path_raw())` where `path_raw()` = `"group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet"`
- `parse_path` does `url.join("group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet")` → `file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet`
- `f.file_url.as_str()` = `"file:///.../group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet"`

So both should match! Why doesn't it work?

Let me check if maybe the issue is with `Path::parse` behavior. In the writer, `Path::parse` is used. But what if `Path::parse` actually decodes percent-encoding?

Actually wait, let me re-read the comment: "Path::from will percent-encode the input, while Path::parse won't."

But what does `Path::parse` do with a string that already has `%20`? It treats it as literal characters. So `Path::parse("group=foo%20bar")` results in a Path with raw parts `["group=foo%20bar"]`.

When `path.to_string()` is called, what does it return? It should return the path as a string with the raw parts joined by `/`. So it would return `"group=foo%20bar/part-00000-xxx.parquet"`.

OK so the Add action's path should have `%20` as literal characters. This should match.

But wait - what if on Windows, the path handling is different? The PR description says "Operating System: Windows". On Windows, `Path::parse` might handle things differently because of backslash vs forward slash.

Actually, let me look at this from a different angle. Let me check if there's an issue with how `snapshot.log_data()` returns the path vs how `view.path_raw()` returns the path.

The `LogDataHandler` wraps `RecordBatch` data that was loaded from the Delta log. But the Delta log data may come from different paths:
1. Directly from the JSON log files
2. From a checkpoint file

Let me check if there's a difference in how paths are handled in these two cases.
